ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific entry for braised mixed mushrooms; 'mushroom food product' (FOODON_03311737) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers cooked mushrooms broadly; no specific code exists for braised mixed mushroom dishes in the FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept identified for braised mixed mushrooms; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical side dish serving of braised mixed mushrooms is approximately 150 g, equivalent to a small bowl or side plate portion as served in a Singaporean zi char or home-cooked meal.

nutrient_source.title: Braised Mixed Mushrooms — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Braised Mixed Mushrooms (cr_id F14020001), confidence 0.95. All per-100g macros and micronutrients are taken from this record without modification.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; mushroom and low-carbohydrate cooked vegetable analogues
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for braised mixed mushrooms. Mushrooms are very low-carbohydrate foods (0.67 g/100g total carbohydrate). GI is estimated at approximately 15 by analogy with cooked mushroom and low-carbohydrate vegetable entries in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2021). Glycemic load per 150 g serving = (15 × 0.67 × 1.50) / 100 = 0.15, which is negligible.

hypertension.guidance: Sodium content of 347 mg/100g places this dish in the borderline-high range for hypertension management; individuals with hypertension should request reduced soy sauce and oyster sauce, limit portion size to 100 g, and pair with potassium-rich foods to partially offset sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate (0.67 g/100g) with negligible glycaemic impact
type2_diabetes.guidance: With only 0.67 g carbohydrate and 0.26 g sugar per 100 g, braised mixed mushrooms have a negligible glycaemic impact and are suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes; the estimated glycaemic load per 150 g serving is 0.15, well below the low-GL threshold of 10.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium at 347 mg/100g partially offset by low saturated fat (0.8 g/100g) and beta-glucan content
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The low saturated fat (0.8 g/100g), zero trans fat, and beta-glucan content of mixed mushrooms support cardiovascular health, but the sodium level of 347 mg/100g warrants caution; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size and request reduced-sodium preparation.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Beta-glucan-rich mushrooms with anti-inflammatory properties; very low saturated fat
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Mixed mushrooms are rich in beta-glucans with immune-modulating and anti-inflammatory properties, and saturated fat is very low at 0.8 g/100g; however, sodium at 347 mg/100g approaches the threshold for RA management and reduced-sodium preparation is recommended.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Braised Mixed Mushrooms is a Singaporean Chinese vegetable side dish consisting of button mushrooms (Agaricus bisporus), golden mushrooms (enoki, Flammulina velutipes), and abalone mushrooms (oyster mushrooms, Pleurotus ostreatus) cooked together in a savoury braising sauce typically made from oyster sauce, light soy sauce, sesame oil, garlic, ginger, and a small amount of cornstarch for thickening. The dish is soft in texture, umami-rich, and mildly savoury with a glossy sauce coating. It is commonly served as a side dish in Singaporean zi char restaurants and home-cooked meals, and is suitable for vegetarians (when oyster sauce is substituted). Nutritionally, it is low in calories (57 kcal/100g), very low in carbohydrates (0.67 g/100g), and contains moderate fat (5.4 g/100g) primarily from cooking oil and sesame oil. Sodium is elevated at 347 mg/100g due to soy and oyster sauces, making portion control important for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is an excellent choice for Type 2 Diabetes management given its negligible glycaemic impact. Mixed mushrooms provide dietary fibre (1.95 g/100g), iron (4.05 mg/100g), calcium (22 mg/100g), and beta-glucans with immune-modulating and anti-inflammatory properties relevant to rheumatoid arthritis management.

Is braised mixed mushrooms suitable for hypertension?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F14020001) with a reported confidence of 0.95. The source is an approved primary regional source for Singaporean dishes. All core macros and key micronutrients are present and internally consistent.
notes: Fields set to -1 (data not available in HPB source): monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g. These detailed fatty acid, vitamin, and moisture data are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry for this dish as of 2026-04-16. Saturated fat (0.8 g/100g) is estimated from the known fat composition of sesame oil and cooking oil used in braising; this is consistent with the HPB source context. Trans fat is reported as 0.0 g (confirmed negligible for plant-based mushroom dish with no hydrogenated fats). GI is an analogous estimate (approximately 15) based on cooked mushroom and low-carbohydrate vegetable entries in the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement exists for this specific dish. The input record listed 'keto' as a dietary tag; this is retained as a cultural/dietary tag but noted: while carbohydrate is very low (0.67 g/100g), fat content (5.4 g/100g) is moderate rather than high, so strict keto classification depends on overall meal composition. Vegan flag is set to false because oyster sauce (derived from oysters) is a standard ingredient; a vegan variant using mushroom sauce is possible but not the canonical preparation.
